rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF SUSIE BAYSANGER JONES
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 7/21/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No notice of the First Report and Account of Successor Administrator has been served. The proof of service filed on 4/29/25 is notice of just the "supplement." (2) The proof of service filed on 4/29/25 does not show the representative capacity of the petitioner in either the caption or in item 1 of the Notice of Hearing. (3) Notice was not given to Lauren Peebles, who filed a Request for Special Notice on 10/28/19. (4) The "supplement" filed on 4/15/25 AMENDED the accounting. [See CRC 7.3 for definitions.] (5) The "revised" account attached to the supplement filed on 4/15/25 is not supported by the "3rd Amended Corrected Final" I&A filed on 4/15/25. (6) The accounting period is for the period 9/12/28 - 1/18/24, which exceeds the two-year period permitted by this Court for a first accounting. [LRSF 14.34 A.] (7) No bank statements have been filed. [LRSF 14.34 D.] Please provide courtesy copies of the petition and an AMENDMENT to the petition (with an amended accounting), plus any other papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner before 7/7/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (A missing proof of service should just be filed, and a missing proposed order should just be lodged or submitted; there is no need to narrate in a pleading that they will be filed...) Except for exhibits, each paper filed with the court must bear a footer in the bottom margin of each page, placed below the page number and DIVIDED FROM THE REST OF THE DOCUMENT PAGE BY A PRINTED LINE. [CRC 2.110(a).] In addition to the requirements of CRC 2.111 and 7.102, all pleadings must also show the name and REPRESENTATIVE CAPACITY, if any, of the party for whom the attorney is appearing, e.g., John Jones, Executor. "Petitioner" or "Respondent" alone is not sufficient. [LRSF 14.15 A.]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TRACEY ANN JONES
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 7/28/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) The amended petition was uploaded on 4/23/25 and e-filed on 4/24/25. Notice mailed on 3/26/25 could not have been for the amended petition; the Notice of Hearing states on its face: "Please refer to the filed documents for more information about the case." [LRSF 14.14 A.] (2) The Notice of Hearing did not show the representative capacity of the petitioner (Administrator) in either the caption or in item 1 and did not have the complete title of the pleading to be heard. (3) See CRC 7.575(b) for computer-generated printouts of bank statements. (4) The administrator says in paragraph 3 of the amended petition that he sold the decedent's residence... but none was inventoried. If there were real property belonging to the decedent, Administrator would have been required to serve notice of proposed action before selling it. [Probate Code Section 10511.] (5) The accounting schedules are not DETAILED. [Probate Code Sect. 1062.] (6) "Interest from old accounts" without dates or sources is insufficient. (7) What "trust" account? (8) Gains and losses must show the calculation, as well as the date(s) of the transaction(s). [Probate Code Sect. 1062(d).] (9) Why were there disbursements to Marin County Superior Court and in the Marin IJ? Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 7/14/25. (Will the other heir not waive an accounting?) Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy Before the court is Delphine Cherry and Jimmy Cherry, Jr.'s Motion to Tax Costs. On April 2, 2025, Petitioner Windy R. Cherry filed her Memorandum of Costs and Disbursements claiming $7,482.43 in costs, following entry of judgment on March 28, 2025. Respondents timely filed a Motion to Tax Costs. The Motion to Tax Costs is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART, as follows. In ruling upon a motion to tax costs, the court's first determination is whether the statute expressly allows the particular item and whether it appears proper on its face. (Rozanova v. Uribe (2021) 68 Cal.App.5th 392, 399, quoting Foothill-DeAnza Community College Dist. v. Emerich (2007) 158 Cal.App.4th 11, 29.) The verified Memorandum of Costs is prima facie evidence of its propriety. (Nelson v. Anderson (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 111, 131.) The burden on a motion to tax costs is on the objecting party, to show the claimed costs are unnecessary or unreasonable. (Id.) The Motion argues the following items are not recoverable costs under Code of Civil Procedure section 1033.5: $935 in filing and motion fees, because several of the costs are duplicative in nature; $1,400 in deposition fees; $400 in service of process fees; $620 in court reporter fees; $617.12 in exhibits; and $492.31 in electronic filing fees. The Motion to tax a portion of the filing fees of $935 is DENIED. The Motion seeks to tax filing fees for either the motion for summary judgment or motion for summary adjudication, and for one of the trust petition's initial filing fees. The Motion argues the separate filing of a motion for summary judgment and a motion for summary adjudication on April 18, 2024, each costing $500, was unreasonable because the motions could have been filed as a single pleading. Although it might be a common practice, the law does not require that a motion for summary judgment and a motion for summary adjudication be filed as one motion or in the alternative. (See Cal. Rules of Court, Rule 3.1350.) Movants cite no authority to the contrary. "Filing, motion, and jury fees" are expressly allowable as costs. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 1033.5(a)(1)). The court finds Movants did not show the claimed motion fees were unreasonable or unnecessary.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] As pleaded in paragraphs 36-38 of the initial petition, the proper venue for the action was Alameda County Superior Court because the principal place of trust administration was in Oakland, California, which is in Alameda County (see Probate Code sec. 17005(a)(1)). On September 9, 2021, Petitioner filed a civil action in San Francisco Superior Court against Respondents, arising out of the same allegations in the trust petition (case number CGC-21-594979). On October 9, 2021, Respondents filed an unlawful detainer action against Petitioner regarding the San Francisco trust real property (case number CUD-21-668280). On July 6, 2022, the Alameda County Superior Court ordered the trust petition transferred to San Francisco County. The trust petition was transferred to this court on April 28, 2023. The second initial filing fee arises from the transfer from Alameda County to San Francisco County, on order of the Alameda County Superior Court. The duplicative fees were the result of an operation of the court, not any unnecessary actions of Petitioner. The Court finds that both initial filing fees were reasonable and necessary in this action. The Motion to tax $1,400 in deposition costs is DENIED. Deposition expenses are expressly recoverable as costs. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 1033.5(a)(3)(A).) "The burden of proof that the deposition was unnecessary or that the costs of taking the deposition were unreasonable is on the party seeking to have that item taxed or reduced." (County of Kern v. Ginn (1983) 146 Cal.App.3d 1107, 1113.) The Motion argues the $2,217.75 cost for Delphine Cherry's deposition was excessive and should be taxed $1,400, because Jimmy Cherry Jr.'s deposition only cost $788.25. The Opposition shows two different deposition companies were hired for the hybrid in-person/Zoom depositions: one for the deposition of Delphine Cherry in Illinois, and one for the deposition of Jimmy Cherry, Jr. in Ohio. Allegations that a cost is unreasonable or unnecessary, without explanation or supporting evidence, do not overcome the verified cost memorandum. (Id.) A difference in costs between the two companies, in two different states, is not itself evidence the cost of deposing Delphine Cherry was unreasonable . Exhibit 1 to the Opposition includes two invoices from the respective companies. The first invoice lists that Jimmie Cherry Jr.'s deposition took approximately 4 hours and cost $556.75 in transcript fees. Also included were the costs of a reporter at $195, and 149 pages of scanned exhibits at $36.50. Delphine Cherry's deposition invoice does not list the deposition's duration, only an "original transcript" cost of $1,723.25. Without detail on the length of Delphine Cherry's deposition it is difficult to determine if the cost of the transcript is "unreasonable" compared to the cost of Jimmy Cherry Jr.'s transcript. Movants offer no information on the deposition's duration. Movants have not carried their burden to show the transcript fees are unreasonable or unnecessary.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] . The invoice for Delphine Cherry's deposition also includes costs for an e-transcript for $35, black and white exhibits for $69.75, color exhibits for $22.75, a conference room for $300, and shipping and handling of $65. $456 of the higher cost for Delphine Cherry's deposition can be attributed to the higher costs of exhibits, and the additional costs of shipping and handling, e-transcript, and conference room usage. Movants have not met their burden of proof that these costs were unreasonable or unnecessary. The Motion to tax $400 for service of process fees is DENIED. The Memorandum of Costs lists two $200 fees for personal service of process on each Respondent on May 26, 2022. Service of process fees are expressly permitted as costs. (See Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 1033.5(a)(4)(B).) The Motion alleges, without support, that personal service on each Respondent was an improper attempt to harass or intimidate them, and unnecessary as they were represented by counsel since August 2021. Movants have not met the burden to show the costs were unreasonable or unnecessary. The Motion to tax $620 in court reporter fees is GRANTED. In the Opposition, Petitioner acknowledges the $620 cost for court reporter fees is not an available cost, as the court did not order the court reporter. The Motion to tax $617.12 for exhibits is DENIED. The Motion incorrectly argues costs of exhibits "prepared at the sole instance" of the party introducing them are not recoverable as costs, citing case law. Such exhibits may be barred as costs if they were not useful to the trier of fact. (Simms v. Los Angeles County, (1950) 35 Cal. 2d. 303,319.) However, photocopies of exhibits are allowed as costs if they were "reasonably helpful to aid the trier of fact," and costs may otherwise be awarded at the trial court's discretion. (See (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1033.5(a)(13) and 1033.5(c)(4).) The exhibits at issue were reasonably helpful to the court in the evidentiary hearing. Movants have not met the burden to show the costs are unreasonable or unnecessary. The Motion to tax $492.31 for electronic filing fees is, DENIED. Electronic filing fees are permitted as costs if they are required by the court. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1033.5(a) (14)). The total amount claimed in the Memorandum of Costs is $492.31. This amount was demonstrated in the Opposition's Exhibit 3. In total, the court taxes Petitioner's memorandum of costs in the amount of $620. Therefore, Petitioner is awarded $6,862.43 in costs.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ROBERT COFFEY
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 6/30/25. Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Receipts that are refunds for amounts disbursed during estate administration or from unknown sources are not includable in the fee base. (2) Please file the seller's closing statement to verify the costs of sale. (3) The estate cannot close until the PG&E stock is sold (and the sale transaction fully reported) or unless it will be distributed pro rata to each beneficiary. (4) Has no further information been found regarding the two large disbursements on 3/14/23? (5) Attorney costs are not sufficiently itemized: they should show the date of each transaction, the payee, the description, and the amount. (6) The Final I&A shows the probate referee's commission as $2,023.35, but the disbursements schedule show $2,024.10 paid. (7) Safra Bank appears to be a New York bank. [Probate Code Section 9700.] Please provide a revised proposed order, if applicable, and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 6/16/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF HOOVER CHARLES LIDDELL JR.
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR SPOUSAL PROPERTY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 7/21/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) The will is a pour-over will to the Liddell Family Trust. Testator intended to dispose of his separate property, if any, and his one-half interest in their community property... by giving them to the trustee of the trust. (2) Page 7 of the will is not for Decedent's will. (3) Attachment 7 does not give any specific dates, such as the date of their marriage or when the account at issue was opened, which would be helpful information. (4) Attachments 7a and 7b both have just the account number without indicating any fractional share; the entire account cannot be determined as having both passed to and belonged to the surviving spouse. Please provide a revised proposed order and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 7/7/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.
